(**MAC**) was an independent [New York State public-benefit corporation](/wiki/New_York_state_public-benefit_corporations "New York state public-benefit corporations") created by the [State of New York](/wiki/State_of_New_York "State of New York") for purposes of providing financing assistance and fiscal oversight of a fiscally-distressed city. Two MACs are explicitly designated under New York law.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

New York State public benefit corporation

A **Municipal Assistance Corporation** (**MAC**) was an independent [New York State public-benefit corporation](/wiki/New_York_state_public-benefit_corporations "New York state public-benefit corporations") created by the [State of New York](/wiki/State_of_New_York "State of New York") for purposes of providing financing assistance and fiscal oversight of a fiscally-distressed city. Two MACs are explicitly designated under New York law.

Best known is the MAC created for [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") during the [1975 New York City fiscal crisis](/wiki/1975_New_York_City_fiscal_crisis "1975 New York City fiscal crisis"). The corporation was born of a recommendation made by a special panel composed of [Simon H. Rifkind](/wiki/Simon_H._Rifkind "Simon H. Rifkind"), [Felix G. Rohatyn](/wiki/Felix_G._Rohatyn "Felix G. Rohatyn"), [Richard M. Shinn](/w/index.php?title=Richard_M._Shinn&action=edit&redlink=1 "Richard M. Shinn (page does not exist)") and [Donald B. Smiley](/w/index.php?title=Donald_B._Smiley&action=edit&redlink=1 "Donald B. Smiley (page does not exist)").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The majority of appointees to the corporation’s board were made by the governor, initially by New York governor [Hugh Carey](/wiki/Hugh_Carey "Hugh Carey"). Members of the MAC included [Donna Shalala](/wiki/Donna_Shalala "Donna Shalala"), later the [United States secretary of health and human services](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Health_and_Human_Services "United States Secretary of Health and Human Services").[[2]](#cite_note-2) As part of the creation of MAC, the state passed legislation that converted the city’s sales and stock transfer taxes into state taxes.[[3]](#cite_note-3) In 2008, having sold almost $10 billion in bonds to keep the city solvent through its worst fiscal crisis, MAC settled its final accounts and voted itself out of existence.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The other MAC was created on July 19, 1995 for the [City of Troy](/wiki/Troy,_New_York "Troy, New York"), which was later terminated on June 27, 2023 after its debt was paid off.[[5]](#cite_note-5)
